title: - CONVERGENCE OF GENERALIZED BELIEF PROPAGA TION ALGORITHM ON GRAPHS WITH MOTIFS [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - CONVERGENCE OF GENERALIZED BELIEF PROPAGA TION ALGORITHM ON GRAPHS WITH MOTIFS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Belief propagation is a fundamental message-passing algorithm for numerous applications in machine learning. It is known that belief propagation algorithm is exact on tree graphs. However, belief propagation is run on loopy graphs in most applications. So, understanding the behavior of belief propagation on loopy graphs has been a major topic for researchers in different areas. In this paper, we study the convergence behavior of generalized belief propagation algorithm on graphs with motifs (triangles, loops, etc.) We show under a certain initialization, generalized belief propagation converges to the global optimum of the Bethe free energy for ferromagnetic Ising models on graphs with motifs.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Undirected graphical models, also known as Markov Random Fields (MRF), provide a framework for modeling high dimensional distributions with dependent variables. Ising models are a special class of discrete pairwise graphical models originated from statistical physics. Ising models have numerous applications in computer vision Ravikumar et al. (2010), bio-informatics Marbach et al. (2012), and social networks Eagle et al. (2009). Explicitly, the joint distribution of Ising model is given by

where _{Xi}i ∈{±_ 1 _}[n]_ are random variables valued in a binary alphabet (also known as "spins"), _Jij_ represents the pairwise interactions between spin _i_ and spin _j_ , _hi_ represents the external field for spin _i_ , _β_ = 1 _/T_ is the reciprocal temperature, and _Z_ is a normalization constant called _partition function_ .

Historically, Ising models are proposed to study ferromagnetism. However, researchers find the computational complexity is the main challenge of performing sampling and inference on Ising models. In the literature, there are multiple ways to tackle the computational complexity. One of the ways are Markov-Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithms. A well-known example is Gibbs sampling, which is a special case of the Metropolis–Hastings algorithm. Basically Gibbs sampling samples a random variable conditioned on the distribution based on the previous samples. It can be shown that Gibbs sampling generates a reversible Markov chain of samples. Thus, the stationary distribution of the Markov chain is the desired joint distribution over the random variables, and it can be reached after the _burn-in period_ . However, it is also well-known that Gibbs sampling will become trapped on multi-modal distribution. For example, Smith and Roberts (1993) and Mengersen (1996) show that when the joint distribution is bi-modal, the Gibbs sampling iterations may be trapped in one of the modes, reducing the probability of convergence.

Another popular way to go around the computational complexity is _variational methods_ , which makes some approximation to the joint distribution. These methods usually turn the inference problem with respect to the approximate joint distribution into some non-convex optimization problem, and solve it either by the standard optimization methods, e.g, gradient descent, or by specialized algorithms like belief propagation. However, due to the non-convexity, those methods usually do not have theoretical guarantees that the solution converges to the global optimum.

Belief propagation (BP) is an effective numerical method for solving inference problems on graphical models. It was originally proposed by Pearl (2014) for tree-like graphs. Ever since it plays a fundamental role in numerous applications including coding theory Frey et al. (1998); Richardson

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

and Urbanke (2001), constraint satisfaction problems Achlioptas and Moore (2006), and community detection in the stochastic block model Decelle et al. (2011). It is well-known that belief propagation is only exact for a model on a graph with locally tree-like structures. The long haunting question is, theoretically how does belief propagation perform on loopy graphs.

We now describe the related work and our contributions.

## **Related work and our contribution**

In a classic work, Yedidia et al. (2003) establishes the connection between belief propagation and the Bethe free energy. He shows that there is one-to-one correspondence between the fixed points of belief propagation and stationary points of the Bethe free energy. Following his work, it is known that the Bethe free energy at the critical points can be represented in terms of fixed point messages of belief propagation Montanari (2013). In a recent work, Koehler (2019) further studies the properties of Bethe free energy at the critical points, and shows for ferromagnetic Ising models, initialized with all one messages, belief propagation converges to the fixed point corresponds to the global maximum of the Bethe free energy. However, those theories consider either asymptotic locally tree-like graphs, or loopy graphs with simple edges. Real technological, social and biological networks have numerous short and large loops and other complex motifs, which lead to non-tree-like structures and essentially loopy graphs with hyper edges. Newman Newman (2009); Karrer and Newman (2010) and Miller (2009) independently propose a model of random graphs with arbitrary distributions of motifs. And Yoon et al. (2011) generalizes the Belief Propagation to graphs with motifs.

Our work builds on generalized belief propagation on graphs with motifs Yoon et al. (2011) and the convergence of belief propagation on ferromagnetic Ising models on loopy graphs with simple edges Koehler (2019). In this paper, we show for ferromagnetic Ising models on graphs with motifs, with all messages initialized to one, generalized belief propagation converges to the fixed point corresponds to the global maximum of the Bethe free energy.

## 2 ISING MODELS ON GRAPHS WITH MOTIFS

Let us introduce the concept of graphs with motifs. In graphs with motifs, each vertex belongs to a given set of motifs. As shown in Fig.1a , different motifs can be attached to vertex _i_ : a simple edge ( _i, j_ ), a triangle, a square, a pentagon, and other non-clique motifs. Graphs with motifs can be viewed as hyper-graphs where motifs play a role of hyper-edges. And the number of specific motifs attached to a vertex is equal to hyper-degree with respect to the specific motifs. In this paper, for simplicity, we only consider simple motifs such as simple edges, and cliques.

Consider the Ising model with arbitrary order of interactions among vertices in each motif on a hyper-graph. Let _Ml_ ( _i_ ) denote a cluster of size _l_ attached to vertex _i_ , where vertices _j_ 1 _, j_ 2 _, . . . , jl−_ 1 together with _i_ form the motif. And let _X_ denote the random variable of spin configurations, the Hamiltonian of the model is

where the first sum corresponds to the external fields at each vertex, the second sum corresponds to the pairwise interactions on simple edges, the third sum corresponds to the higher order interactions among spins in triangles, the fourth sum corresponds to the higher order interactions among spins in squares, and so on. As discussed in the previous section, most previous works focus on Ising models with pairwise interactions. In this paper, we are interested in Ising models with higher order interactions. For simplicity, we consider Ising models with only external fields and higher order interactions in triangles. Our derivation can be extended to more general cases.
Consider Ising models with only external fields and higher order interactions in triangles, the Hamiltonian of the model is
where ( _i, j, k_ ) is a triangle, which can also be denoted as _M_ 3( _i_ ), _M_ 3( _j_ ), or _M_ 3( _k_ ).

By Boltzmann’s law, the joint distribution is defined by

where _Z_ is the _partition function_ .

Throughout this paper, we focus on _ferromagnetic_ Ising models, which is defined below **Definition 1.** _An Ising model is ferromagnetic if Jijk ≥_ 0 _for all triangle motifs_ ( _i, j, k_ ) _and hi ≥_ 0 _for all i._

We introduce a intermediate message _µM_ 3( _i_ ) from a motif _M_ 3( _i_ ) to spin _i_ .

In the literature, different works have different definitions of messages. _µM_ 3( _i_ ) is not the message definition we eventually work with in this paper, but it helps to understand the connections between different works. So, abusing the terminology a little bit, we call it ‘intermediate message’.

By the definition of generalized Belief Propagation, the probability that spin _i_ is in a state _Xi_ is determined by the normalized product of incoming intermediate messages from motifs attached to spin _i_ and the external field factor _e[βh][i][X][i]_ ,

where _A_ is a normalization constant. And the belief update rule is given by:

where _E_ ( _M_ 3( _i_ )) is an energy of the interaction among spins in the triangle _M_ 3( _i_ ), and _B_ is a normalization constant.
Multiplying Equation (7) by _Xi_ and summing over all spin configurations, we obtain an equation for the effective field _λM_ 3( _i_ ),

More specifically, if the motif _M_ 3( _i_ ) is a triangle ( _i, j, k_ ), the message can be alternatively represented as _νi→M_ 3( _i_ ) = _νi→jk_ = tanh( _λM_ 3( _i_ )). From now on, let the reciprocal temperature _β_ = 1, we can further simplify Equation (8) as

## 3 BETHE FREE ENERGY OF HIGHER ORDER ISING MODELS

In order to get the Bethe free energy of our higher order Ising model (3), we need to go through the Gibbs variational principle as Yedidia et al. (2003) did for standard Ising models with pairwise interactions. Let _P[∗]_ be a joint distribution defined by our model (4). If we have some approximate joint distribution _P_ , from Gibbs variational principle, we can write Gibbs free energy as

where _U_ ( _P_ ) is called the _average energy_ , and _S_ ( _P_ ) is the _entropy_ .

We would like to derive a Gibbs free energy that is a function of both the one-node beliefs _Pi_ ( _xi_ ) and the three-node beliefs _Pijk_ ( _xi, xj, xk_ ). The beliefs should satisfy the normalization conditions and the marginalization conditions. In other words, _P_ lies in the following polytope of locally consistent distributions

_Pi_ ( _xi_ ) _, Pijk_ ( _xi, xj, xk_ ) _≥_ 0 for all triangles ( _i, j, k_ ), and all _xi, xj, xk_

Because we only consider external fields and higher order interactions with triangles in our model, the one-node and three-node beliefs are actually sufficient to determine the average energy. For our model (3) and for any approximate joint probability _P_ such that one-node marginal probabilities are _Pi_ ( _xi_ ) and the three-node marginal probabilities are _Pijk_ ( _xi, xj, xk_ ), the average energy will have the form

The average energy computed with the true marginal probabilities _Pi[∗]_[(] _[x][i]_[)][ and] _[ P][ ∗] ijk_[(] _[x][i][, x][j][, x][k]_[)][ will] also have this form, so if one-node and three-node beliefs are exact, the average energy given by Equation (16) will be exact.

For computing the entropy, we usually need an approximation. We can compute the entropy exactly if we can explicitly express the joint distribution _P_ ( _x_ ) in terms of the one-node and three-node beliefs. If our graph were tree-like hyper-graph with triangle motifs only (see Fig. 1b as an example), we can in fact do that. In that case, we can represent the joint probability distribution in the form

where _qi_ is the hyper-degree of node _i_ .

Using Equation (17), we get the Bethe approximation to the entropy as

Combining Equation (16) and (18), we obtain the Bethe free energy

Notice when the hyper-graph is a tree, the Bethe free energy _G_ Bethe( _P_ ) will have the correct functional dependence on the beliefs. And solving the optimization problem: maximizing _G_ Bethe( _P_ ) over the polytope of locally consistent distribution (15) will give the true marginals. For loopy hyper-graphs, the Bethe free energy _G_ Bethe( _P_ ) is only an approximation, which is the essence of the variational methods.

We can derive the BP equations from the first-order optimality conditions for the aforementioned optimization problem. In other words, we can verify that _a set of beliefs gives a BP fixed point in any hyper-graph if and only if they are stationary points of the Bethe free energy_ for the generalized BP. To see this, we need to add Lagrange multipliers to _G_ Bethe( _P_ ) to form a Lagrangian _L_ . We define the set of _pre-fixpoints_ and _post-fixpoints_ messages similar as in Koehler (2019):
From Lemma 2, we know _S_ post is a convex set, while _S_ pre is typically non-convex and even disconnected. Next, we show the gradient of the dual Bethe free energy is well-behaved on these sets:

Recall _φ_ ( _ν_ ) _i→jk_ is the updated message from spin _i_ to motif _{j, k}_ based on the current messages _ν_ . If _ν ∈ S_ pre or _S_ post, then the signs of the gradient of Bethe free energy are determined by Equation (66) as claimed.

Based on Lemma 2 and 3, we can prove our main theorem.

**Theorem 1.** _Suppose that generalized BP is run from initial messages νi_[(0)] _→jk_[=][1] _[ and there is at] least one fixed point in_ [ _x[∗] ,_ 1) _[n] . The messages converge to a fixed point ν[∗] of the generalized BP equations such that for any other fixed point µ, µi→jk ≤ νi[∗] →jk[.][Furthermore]_

> _Proof._ If there is at least one fixed point in [ _x[∗] ,_ 1) _[n]_ , and the initialization is _νi_[(0)] _→jk_[=][1][for][all] hyper-edges _{i, j, k}_ . For each iteration of Belief Propagation, _ν_[(] _[t]_[)] = _φ_ ( _ν_[(] _[t][−]_[1)] ).

From Lemma 2, we know _φ_ is monotonic on [ _x[∗] ,_ 1) _[n]_ . So, _ν_[(0)] _, ν_[(1)] _, . . . , ν_[(] _[t]_[)] is a coordinate-wise decreasing sequence, which will converge to some fixed point. By monotonicity, we see that for any fixed point _µ ∈_ [ _x[∗] ,_ 1) _[n]_ , _µi→jk ≤ νi_[(] _→[t]_[)] _jk_[for all] _[ t]_[.][Hence, it holds for] _[ ν][∗]_[as well.]

Finally, consider any other point _ν ∈ S_ post, by convexity of _S_ post, we know that the line segment from _ν_ to _ν[∗]_ is entirely contained in _S_ post. By Lemma 3, we see that for any point _x_ on this interpolating line segment that _∇G_ Bethe( _ν_ ) _·_ ( _ν[∗] − ν_ ) _≥_ 0, and integrating over this line segment gives us _G_ Bethe( _ν_ ) _≤ G_ Bethe( _ν[∗]_ ).

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper attempts to extend convergence results for Belief Propagation (BP) on ferromagnetic Ising models to Generalized Belief Propagation (GBP) on hypergraphs (graphs with motifs). While the motivation and the high-level connection between GBP fixed points and Bethe free energy are well-established in the literature, this manuscript contains significant technical gaps and mathematical errors. The most critical issues reside in the derivation of the 'dual' Bethe free energy and the proof of Lemma 2/Lemma 3. Specifically, the authors appear to have copied segments of proofs from Koehler (2019) that apply to pairwise Ising models without properly adapting them to the higher-order interaction structures (triangles) used in this paper. This leads to inconsistencies where the formulas used for gradients and concavity do not match the defined Hamiltonian or the GBP messages.

comments.title: Mathematical Mismatch in Hamiltonian and Belief Equations
comments.quote: H(x) = -\sum_{i} h_i X_i - \sum_{(i,j,k)} J_{ijk} X_i X_j X_k \dots \tanh^{-1}(\nu_{i \to jk}) = \beta h_i + \sum_{M \in \partial i \setminus jk} \tanh^{-1}(\lambda_M(i))
comments.explanation: In Section 2, the authors define a Hamiltonian with triple interactions (triangles). However, Equation (12) and the subsequent derivations attempt to use a message-passing form that mimics pairwise BP. In a triangle motif interaction $J_{ijk}X_i X_j X_k$, the message from a motif to a node depends on the beliefs of the other two nodes in a non-linear way that doesn't simplify to a single $\tanh^{-1}$ sum as easily as the pairwise case. The transition from Equation (7) to (12) skips the non-trivial marginalization over *two* variables $(j, k)$ simultaneously, and the notation $\tanh^{-1}(\nu)$ suggests a pairwise structure that contradicts the defined $J_{ijk}$ interaction.

comments.title: Incorrect Dual Bethe Free Energy Formulation
comments.quote: G^*_{Bethe}(\nu) = \sum_{(i,j,k)} F_{ijk}(\lambda) - \sum_i (q_i - 1) F_i(\lambda_i)
comments.explanation: In Lemma 1 and Corollary 1, the authors attempt to formulate the dual Bethe free energy. In Equation (46) and (49), they define $F_{ijk}$ and $F_i$. However, the coefficients ($q_i - 1$) and the structure of the sum are characteristic of the Bethe approximation for pairwise graphs (edges). For hypergraphs with motifs (triangles), the counting coefficients (often called overcounting factors in GBP) must be derived using the Junction Tree algorithm or the Region Graph Method. Simply replacing 'edges' with 'triangles' in the pairwise formula generally does not yield a valid dual free energy for GBP, as it fails to account for the overlap correctly.

comments.title: Invalid Proof for Lemma 2 (Copy-Paste Error)
comments.quote: Suppose that f(x) = \tanh(h + \sum_{(i,j)} \tanh^{-1}(x_i x_j)) ... Note for any non-negative vector w, if we let ... C = q_i w w_j ...
comments.explanation: Lemma 2 is used to prove concavity and monotonicity. However, the function $f(x)$ defined in Equation (52) is strictly for a pairwise Ising model (it involves terms like $x_i x_j$). The paper is supposedly about triangle motifs $J_{ijk}$, which should involve terms with three variables. Furthermore, the variables $q$ and $w$ used in the derivation from Equation (56) to (61) are not defined in the context of this paper, but appear to be copied identically from a proof in Koehler (2019) regarding standard BP. Because the functional form of GBP updates for triangles differs fundamentally from pairwise BP, this lemma does not actually prove the properties for the model described in the paper.

comments.title: Gradient Formula Inconsistency
comments.quote: \frac{\partial G^*_{Bethe}}{\partial \lambda_{i \to jk}} = \phi(\nu)_{i \to jk} - \nu_{i \to jk}
comments.explanation: Equation (66) claims the gradient of the dual free energy is the difference between the message and its update. While this holds for standard BP on pairwise models (as shown by Yedidia and Koehler), it is a non-trivial identity that depends on the specific form of the Bethe entropy. Given that the entropy approximation for motifs [Eq. (18)] and the Hamiltonian [Eq. (3)] are higher-order, one cannot assume this simple gradient relation holds without a rigorous derivation specific to the region graph/GBP being used. If the dual free energy in Lemma 1 is incorrectly formulated (as noted above), this gradient result is also likely incorrect.

comments.title: Ambiguity of Region Graph Construction
comments.quote: Generalized Belief Propagation ... we only consider simple motifs such as simple edges, and cliques.
comments.explanation: The paper uses the term 'Generalized Belief Propagation' but doesn't explicitly define the region graph or the hierarchy of clusters used. This is critical because the convergence and the form of the free energy depend entirely on which regions are chosen and how their beliefs are constrained. While Figure 1b suggests a specific 'tree-like hypergraph', the methodology is underspecified for a general 'loopy' graph with motifs, making reproduction of the result impossible.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling theoretical result on the convergence of generalized belief propagation (GBP) for ferromagnetic Ising models on graphs with motifs, extending prior work on tree-like and simple loopy graphs. The core argument builds logically from variational principles to message-passing dynamics, leveraging properties of concavity and monotonicity in the dual Bethe free energy. The derivation of the dual Bethe free energy and its gradient behavior is mostly sound, and the connection between fixed points of GBP and stationary points of the Bethe free energy is well-motivated. However, there are several technical issues in notation, consistency, and justification that reduce confidence in the completeness of the proof, particularly around the role of higher-order motifs and the monotonicity argument in Lemma 2. While the overall structure and intent are solid, the presentation suffers from ambiguities and gaps in key derivations.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of effective field in Equation (9)
comments.quote:
Multiplying Equation (7) by _Xi_ and summing over all spin configurations, we obtain an equation for the effective field _λM_ 3( _i_ ), 

where
comments.explanation: Equation (9) defines _λM_3(i)_ via a sum over _X_, but the right-hand side depends on _Xi_, while the left-hand side is labeled as a scalar effective field. This suggests _λM_3(i)_ should be state-dependent, but later usage (e.g., in tanh) treats it as a scalar. From context, it appears the sum over _X_ is meant to compute an expectation, but no normalization or conditioning is shown. A correct derivation would involve taking expectations or deriving consistency conditions under factorization assumptions. The expression as written is ambiguous and does not yield a scalar unless additional constraints are imposed. The reference to Yoon et al. (2011) does not resolve this, as a standalone paper should clarify such a central quantity. This undermines the foundation of the message definition in Equation (12).

comments.title: Incorrect gradient sign in Lemma 3 and its proof
comments.explanation: The claim in Lemma 3 asserts that the gradient of the dual Bethe free energy is non-positive on _S_pre_ and non-negative on _S_post_. However, standard results in variational inference (e.g., in Wainwright & Jordan) show that belief propagation corresponds to coordinate ascent on the Bethe free energy, meaning that the update direction should align with the gradient. If _ν_ is a pre-fixpoint (ν ≤ φ(ν)), then the update φ(ν) − ν ≥ 0 implies the gradient should be positive, not negative. The claimed sign is therefore reversed. The proof cites Equation (66), which is not included in the text, so the error may lie in an omitted derivation. Given that the main theorem relies on this gradient behavior to conclude optimality, the reversal calls the conclusion into question unless corrected.

comments.title: Ambiguous domain and monotonicity in Lemma 2
comments.quote: _Suppose that f_ ( _x_ ) = tanh( _h_ +[] ( _i,j_ )[tanh] _[−]_[1][(] _[x][i][x][j]_[))] _[for][any][h][≥]_[0] _[.][Then][f][is][a] concave monotone function on the domain_ [ _x[∗] ,_ 1) _[n] ._
comments.explanation: The function f is defined with a summation over (i,j), but it is unclear whether this is a vector-valued function or a component-wise update. The notation suggests f(x) depends on all x_i, but the domain is [x*,1)^n, implying a vector input. However, in GBP, messages are updated per hyperedge, not per node. The claim that f is monotonic and concave on a product domain requires clarification: is this component-wise monotonicity? The proof uses a function g(x) without defining its domain or relation to f. Crucially, the summation index (i,j) is not properly scoped—likely a typo where it should be over neighbors in the motif. While the intent appears to be to mimic Koehler’s argument, the current version lacks rigor and is not reproducible.

comments.title: Missing factor in dual Bethe free energy expression
comments.quote:
**Lemma 1.** _The dual Bethe free energy at a critical point can be defined by_ 

_where_ 

_Proof._ Recall the Bethe free energy 

By rearranging terms, we have 

where 

and 

_Fijk_ ( _λ_ ) = E[ _JijkXiXjXk_ + _λ[′] i→jk[X][i]_[+] _[ λ][′] j→ik[X][j]_[+] _[ λ][′] k→ij[X][k]_[] +] _[ H]_[(] _[X][i][, X][j][, X][k]_[)]
comments.explanation: The expression for _Fijk(λ)_ includes an undefined term _H(Xi, Xj, Xk)_, which is not present in the Hamiltonian (3) or anywhere else. The original Hamiltonian includes only external fields and triangle interactions. If _H_ is meant to represent the external field contribution, it should be split per node and not duplicated across _Fijk_. Moreover, each external field _hiXi_ appears in multiple triangles if node _i_ is in multiple motifs, so including it fully in each _Fijk_ would overcount. The correct dual form should account for degree or use node-based potentials. This overcounting leads to an incorrect dual energy, affecting all subsequent analysis, including Lemma 3 and Theorem 1. The error is fundamental to the variational formulation.

comments.title: Underspecified message initialization and domain in Theorem 1
comments.quote: _Suppose that generalized BP is run from initial messages νi_[(0)] _→jk_[=][1] _[ and there is at] least one fixed point in_ [ _x[∗] ,_ 1) _[n] .
comments.explanation: The theorem assumes initialization at νi→jk = 1, which corresponds to tanh⁻¹(1) = ∞ in the effective field, leading to numerical instability. While the limit may be handled formally, in practice tanh maps to (-1,1), so ν = 1 is not in the interior of the domain. The paper does not clarify how this boundary case is treated in updates or whether the function φ is well-defined at ν=1. Koehler’s work typically initializes at ν=0 or small values. Initializing at 1 may trivially satisfy ν ≥ φ(ν) if φ is bounded, making the decreasing sequence property vacuous. The choice needs justification, especially since convergence behavior at boundaries can differ drastically.

comments.title: Incorrect use of tanh and inverse tanh without domain checks
comments.quote: Define _νi→jk_ := tanh( _λ[′] i→jk_[)][, we have]
comments.explanation: Throughout the paper, the transformation ν = tanh(λ') is used freely, implying that λ' ∈ ℝ maps to ν ∈ (-1,1). However, in the consistency equations (e.g., Equation 12), ν appears inside tanh⁻¹, which is only defined for |ν| < 1. When the paper initializes ν=1, tanh⁻¹(1) is infinite, breaking the expressions. While this might be interpreted as a limit, the analysis does not treat it rigorously. Moreover, in Lemma 2, the domain [x*,1)^n approaches 1, where tanh⁻¹ diverges, affecting the gradient and concavity arguments. The paper should either restrict ν to (-1,1) or handle the boundary explicitly.

comments.title: Claimed global optimality not fully justified by current arguments
comments.quote: generalized belief propagation converges to the fixed point corresponds to the global maximum of the Bethe free energy
comments.explanation: The abstract and contribution section claim convergence to the global maximum of the Bethe free energy. However, Theorem 1 only shows convergence to the largest fixed point in the domain [x*,1)^n, and that G_Bethe(ν) ≤ G_Bethe(ν*) for ν in S_post. But this does not establish global optimality over all possible beliefs or messages, only within a restricted set. The Bethe free energy can have multiple critical points, and without showing convexity or uniqueness, the claim of global optimality is too strong. The result is better described as convergence to a particular fixed point that dominates others in the monotonicity order. This is a significant overstatement given the analysis.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper extends convergence results for Belief Propagation to graphs with motifs (specifically triangles) for ferromagnetic Ising models. The authors claim that with all messages initialized to 1, Generalized Belief Propagation converges to the global optimum of the Bethe free energy. While the problem is interesting and the approach shows promise, there are significant gaps in the theoretical exposition. Most critically, the proof of Lemma 3 references a missing Equation (66) that is essential for establishing the gradient properties, and Theorem 1 assumes the existence of a fixed point in a specific interval without providing conditions for this assumption. Additionally, the global optimality claim is not fully justified, as the authors only establish optimality on a subset of the message space. The paper would benefit from a more complete and rigorous proof of the main theorem and clearer discussion of the practical implications of the initialization condition.

comments.title: Missing Equation (66) in proof of Lemma 3
comments.quote:
Recall $\phi(
u)_{i	o jk}$ is the updated message from spin $i$ to motif ${j, k}$ based on the current messages $
u$. If $
u \in S_{	ext{pre}}$ or $S_{	ext{post}}$, then the signs of the gradient of Bethe free energy are determined by Equation (66) as claimed.
comments.explanation: The proof of Lemma 3 explicitly references Equation (66) to determine the signs of the gradient, but this equation is not provided anywhere in the text. This is a critical gap because Lemma 3 is used to establish the optimality properties of the fixed point in Theorem 1. Without seeing Equation (66), readers cannot verify the claims about gradient signs, which undermines the entire convergence argument. This appears to be more than an OCR issue, as the surrounding equations are numbered sequentially and there's no gap where Equation (66) would naturally fit.

comments.title: Unjustified assumption about existence of fixed point
comments.quote: Suppose that generalized BP is run from initial messages $\nu_{i\to jk}^{(0)} = 1$ and there is at least one fixed point in $[x^*, 1)^n$.
comments.explanation: Theorem 1 assumes the existence of at least one fixed point in the interval $[x^*, 1)^n$, but the authors provide no conditions under which this assumption holds. For ferromagnetic Ising models on tree-like graphs, it's known that there's a unique fixed point with all positive messages. However, for loopy graphs with motifs, the existence of such a fixed point is not guaranteed. This assumption significantly limits the theorem's applicability, as readers don't know when the result can be applied. The authors should either provide sufficient conditions for the existence of such a fixed point or clearly state this as a limitation.

comments.title: Gap in proof of global optimality
comments.quote:
Finally, consider any other point $
u \in S_{	ext{post}}$, by convexity of $S_{	ext{post}}$, we know that the line segment from $
u$ to $
u^*$ is entirely contained in $S_{	ext{post}}$. By Lemma 3, we see that for any point $x$ on this interpolating line segment that $
abla G_{	ext{Bethe}}(
u) \cdot (
u^* - 
u) \geq 0$, and integrating over this line segment gives us $G_{	ext{Bethe}}(
u) \leq G_{	ext{Bethe}}(
u^*)$.
comments.explanation: The authors show that $\nu^*$ is a maximum of the Bethe free energy on $S_{\text{post}}$, but they don't explain why this implies that $\nu^*$ is a global maximum of the Bethe free energy overall. This is a significant gap in the proof, as the main claim of the paper is that GBP converges to the global optimum of the Bethe free energy. The authors need to show that either (1) any fixed point of GBP must be in $S_{\text{post}}$, or (2) the global maximum of the Bethe free energy is always attained in $S_{\text{post}}$. Without this connection, the proof doesn't establish the claimed global optimality.

comments.title: Non-standard initialization condition
comments.quote: Suppose that generalized BP is run from initial messages $\nu_{i\to jk}^{(0)} = 1$
comments.explanation: The authors initialize all messages to 1, which corresponds to infinite effective fields (since $\nu = \tanh(\lambda)$ and $\tanh^{-1}(1) = \infty$). This is a non-standard initialization that might not be practical in real applications. The authors should discuss the implications of this initialization and whether alternative initializations (e.g., messages close to but not exactly 1) would also lead to convergence to the global optimum. Without this discussion, it's unclear how practitioners should apply this result.

comments.title: Limited scope of the result
comments.quote: For simplicity, we consider Ising models with only external fields and higher order interactions in triangles. Our derivation can be extended to more general cases.
comments.explanation: The authors focus on Ising models with only external fields and triangle interactions, which significantly limits the applicability of their result. While they mention that their derivation can be extended to more general cases (other motifs, higher-order interactions), they provide no details on how this extension would work. Given that real-world graphs often have more complex motifs, this is a significant limitation. A brief discussion or appendix outlining how the approach could be extended to general motifs would be valuable.

comments.title: Incomplete convergence argument in Theorem 1
comments.quote:
From Lemma 2, we know $\phi$ is monotonic on $[x^*, 1)^n$. So, $
u^{(0)}, 
u^{(1)}, \ldots, 
u^{(t)}$ is a coordinate-wise decreasing sequence, which will converge to some fixed point.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the sequence of messages converges to a fixed point because it's coordinate-wise decreasing, but this argument is incomplete. A decreasing sequence in $\mathbb{R}^n$ doesn't necessarily converge to a fixed point of the iteration function $\phi$ - it could converge to a point outside the domain where $\phi$ is defined, or it might not converge at all if the sequence is not bounded below. The authors should provide a more rigorous convergence argument, perhaps using the monotone convergence theorem or establishing a contraction property.

comments.title: Unclear definition of pre-fixpoints and post-fixpoints
comments.quote: We define the set of pre-fixpoints and post-fixpoints messages similar as in Koehler (2019):
comments.explanation: The authors define $S_{\text{pre}}$ and $S_{\text{post}}$ without clearly explaining their significance or properties. While they reference Koehler (2019) for the definitions, a self-contained explanation would be helpful. For instance, why are these sets important for the analysis? What properties do they have? How do they relate to the fixed points of GBP? Without this context, readers may struggle to follow the optimization landscape analysis.

comments.title: Undefined notation for hyper-degree
comments.quote: where $q_i$ is the hyper-degree of node $i$.
comments.explanation: The authors use $q_i$ to denote the hyper-degree of node $i$, but they don't clearly define this notation. While readers familiar with hyper-graphs might understand this term, a clear definition would be helpful. Specifically, is $q_i$ the total number of motifs containing node $i$, or is it the number of motifs of a specific type? This clarification would make the paper more accessible to readers outside the immediate subfield.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper attempts to extend the convergence analysis of Belief Propagation (BP) for ferromagnetic Ising models from loopy graphs with simple edges to graphs with more complex motifs (specifically triangles). While the ambition is clear and the high-level strategy—adapting the approach of Koehler (2019) to generalized BP—is sound, the presentation suffers from significant notation slippage, gaps in formal derivation, and ambiguity in the definition of the update rules and their relationship to the Bethe free energy. The lack of clarity regarding the transition from the general formulation to the specific triangle case makes it difficult to verify the core results.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of message update rule
comments.quote: where E ( M 3 ( i ) ) is an energy of the interaction among spins in the triangle M 3 ( i ) , and B is a normalization constant. Multiplying Equation (7) by X i and summing over all spin configurations, we obtain an equation for the effective field λ M 3 ( i ) ... where ... Equation (12) is the consistency equation for fixed point hyper-edge messages ν i [ ∗ ] →jk
comments.explanation: Equation (7) introduces an update rule that lacks an explicit functional definition for the 'energy' term or the normalization constant B. Furthermore, the leap between this generalized belief update and the specific hyper-edge message Equation (12) is opaque. Specifically, it is unclear how the 'intermediate message' concept defined in Section 2 relates to the specific tanh-parameterization of the hyper-edge messages. This makes the derivation of the fixed-point iteration for the triangle case underspecified.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Bethe free energy Lagrangian multipliers
comments.quote: Setting λ [ ′ ] i→jk = λ i→jk (1) − 2 λ i→jk ( − 1) , we find that at a critical point of the Lagrangian that ... And the equation ∂ P ∂ L i ( x i ) [ = 0 ] gives: Setting λ [ ′ ] i→jk = λ i→jk (1) − 2 λ i→jk ( − 1) , we find that at a critical point of the Lagrangian that
comments.explanation: The text repeats the exact same algebraic assignment for two different partial derivatives of the Lagrangian (one with respect to Pijk and one with respect to Pi). It is highly improbable that the same linear combination of Lagrange multipliers satisfies both the stationarity conditions for the 3-node belief and the 1-node belief simultaneously without further justification. The document fails to explicitly define the relationship between the different sets of multipliers.

comments.title: Underspecified gradient calculation
comments.quote: The lemma will follow if we compute the gradient of the dual Bethe free energy function G [ ∗ ] Bethe ( ν ) . ... If ν ∈ S pre or S post, then the signs of the gradient of Bethe free energy are determined by Equation (66) as claimed.
comments.explanation: The paper references an 'Equation (66)', but only goes up to (52). More importantly, the proof for Lemma 3 is missing; it merely asserts that the gradient of the dual Bethe free energy has a specific sign based on an unprovided calculation. Given that the convergence property relies entirely on the landscape of this function, the lack of this derivation is a critical gap.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in update function
comments.quote: Alternatively, we denote Equation (12) as ν i→jk = φ ( ν ) i→jk .
comments.explanation: The notation suggests φ represents a vector-valued function, but the subscript 'i→jk' implies a scalar component. This inconsistency continues into Lemma 2 and 3 where φ is treated as a component-wise operator on a vector ν. While standard in loose literature, in a paper relying on monotonicity proofs, the lack of distinction between the operator φ(ν) and its component φ_i→jk(ν) creates ambiguity about the domain of the Monotonicity claimed.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper attempts to extend convergence results for generalized belief propagation to ferromagnetic Ising models on graphs with motifs (triangles), building on prior work by Koehler and Yoon et al. However, the technical execution is critically flawed. The core BP update equation is missing, making the central function φ undefined. Lemma 2, which is key to the monotonicity and convexity arguments, addresses a pairwise BP update and does not incorporate the triangle interactions central to the model. The proofs are incomplete or garbled, and the main theorem relies on unverified assumptions about existence and monotonicity. The paper as presented lacks the necessary rigor and contains logical gaps that undermine its claims.

comments.title: Missing equation for BP update (Equation 12)
comments.quote:
From now on, let the reciprocal temperature β = 1, we can further simplify Equation (8) as 
where ∂i denotes the motifs attached to spin i. Equation (12) is the consistency equation for fixed point hyper-edge messages ν[∗] i→jk of the generalized belief propagation.
comments.explanation: The actual update equation for ν_{i→jk} is not displayed. This equation defines the mapping φ that is central to all subsequent analysis (e.g., Lemma 2, gradient computations, fixed points). Without it, the reader cannot verify the correctness of the lemmas or the application of techniques from Koehler (2019). While OCR may have dropped the equation, the text as provided is incomplete.

comments.title: Lemma 2 does not match the triangle-interaction model
comments.quote: Lemma 2. Suppose that f(x) = tanh( h + Σ_{(i,j)} tanh^{-1}(x_i x_j) ) for any h ≥ 0. Then f is a concave monotone function on the domain [x*,1)^n.
comments.explanation: The function f in Lemma 2 corresponds to standard pairwise belief propagation (edges only). The model studied in the paper includes triplet interactions J_{ijk} X_i X_j X_k, which should appear inside the tanh as terms like tanh^{-1}(θ_{ijk} ν_{j→i} ν_{k→i}). The paper provides no justification that φ (the actual update for the motif model) reduces to this pairwise form. Thus Lemma 2 is irrelevant to the setting and cannot be used to establish properties of φ.

comments.title: Incomplete and erroneous proof of Lemma 2
comments.explanation: The proof of Lemma 2 is presented in a garbled manner. It treats the multivariate function as if it were univariate, the derivative expressions are not clearly defined, and the derivation of the threshold x* is sketchy. Even if the lemma were correct for the pairwise case, the provided proof is insufficient to convince a careful reader.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claims about convexity of S_post based on wrong function
comments.quote: From Lemma 2, we know S_post is a convex set, while S_pre is typically non-convex and even disconnected.
comments.explanation: The definitions of S_pre and S_post depend on the mapping φ, which is not properly defined and does not match the function f from Lemma 2. Hence the convexity claim is unsupported. The paper does not analyze the actual φ for the motif model.

comments.title: Theorem 1 relies on unproven assumptions about existence and monotonicity
comments.quote: Suppose that generalized BP is run from initial messages νi(0)→jk = 1 and there is at least one fixed point in [x*,1)^n.
comments.explanation: The theorem assumes without proof that a fixed point exists in the specified interval. More critically, the monotonicity of the iteration (needed for convergence to the largest fixed point) is not established for the actual φ with triangle interactions. Lemma 2, which might have been intended to show monotonicity, is about a different function. Thus the claimed convergence result is unjustified.

comments.title: Dual Bethe free energy expression may contain sign or factor errors
comments.explanation: The dual Bethe free energy in Corollary 1 includes terms like log(1 + θ_{ijk} ν_{i→jk} ν_{j→ik} ν_{k→ij}) and log(1 − θ_{ijk} ...) without a clear derivation from the Hamiltonian (3). Given the sign convention for ferromagnetic interactions (J_{ijk} ≥ 0) and the definition of θ = tanh(J_{ijk}), the signs need careful checking to ensure the free energy matches the Bethe approximation. The paper does not provide the intermediate steps from Lemma 1 to Corollary 1, leaving the correctness ambiguous.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper investigates the convergence behavior of Generalized Belief Propagation (GBP) for Ising models on graphs containing complex motifs, such as triangles. The theoretical contribution, extending existing results on simple graphs to hyper-graph structures, is sound in spirit. However, the implementation of the mathematical analysis presents two significant issues. First, a critical mathematical inconsistency appears in the derivation of the dual Bethe free energy, where the coefficients of the terms associated with triangle motifs are asymmetric. Second, there is a terminological conflict between the abstract's claim of finding a 'global optimum' of the Bethe free energy and the proof's demonstration of convergence to a 'global maximum', which introduces confusion regarding the nature of the solution (minimization vs. maximization of the objective).
